question: [File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the mirror node to record a reorganized transaction history by supplying a collection where a single node with stake >= stakeRequiredForConsensus has its signature marked VERIFIED with a fraudulent fileHash, since validate() only requires that the total stake of a hash group meets the threshold and does not require a minimum number of distinct nodes?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getStakeRequiredForConsensus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the RoundingMode.CEILING in getStakeRequiredForConsensus() combined with a carefully chosen totalStake value to produce a stakeRequiredForConsensus that is exactly equal to the stake of a fraudulent hash group, allowing a minority-stake attacker to pass the >= comparison in canReachConsensus() with the minimum possible stake?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the mirror node to accept a tampered stream file by exploiting the fact that the validate() method does not verify that the filename captured from the first signature matches the filenames of all other signatures in the collection, allowing a mixed-filename collection to pass validation with the wrong file being downloaded?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a history reorganization by exploiting the fact that the signatureHashMap is a HashMultimap (not a LinkedHashMultimap), so the iteration order of keySet() is determined by hash codes of the fileHashAsHex strings, and an attacker who can predict or control the hash code of a fraudulent fileHashAsHex string can ensure it is iterated before the legitimate hash, causing the fraudulent group to be evaluated first?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that validate() does not enforce a minimum number of distinct nodes required for consensus (only a stake threshold), so that in a network where staking is not activated and the fallback count-based mode is used (ConsensusNodeWrapper.getStake() returns 1 per node), a single node's signature is sufficient if totalStake equals 1, allowing a single compromised node to reorganize history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the mirror node to accept a tampered stream file by exploiting the zero-stake fallback behavior where ConsensusNodeWrapper.getTotalStake() returns nodeCount when totalStake is 0, and if the attacker can reduce the apparent nodeCount (e.g., by causing nodes to be removed from the address book), the consensus threshold drops and a smaller fraudulent group can pass?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a history reorganization by exploiting the fact that validate() processes all VERIFIED signatures regardless of whether they belong to nodes that are currently active in the address book, allowing signatures from decommissioned or removed nodes to contribute stake to a fraudulent hash group?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the mirror node to record a reorganized transaction history by exploiting the fact that the validate() method does not check the timestamp ordering of stream files, so that a fraudulent signature collection for an older stream file can be submitted after the current head, causing the mirror node to re-process and overwrite already-ingested transaction records?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method mutates the status of StreamFileSignature objects in-place (validatedSignatures.forEach(s -> s.setStatus(CONSENSUS_REACHED))) and these objects are shared references, so that if the same StreamFileSignature object appears in multiple concurrent validate() calls, a race condition could cause a fraudulent signature to be marked CONSENSUS_REACHED?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the mirror node to accept a tampered stream file by exploiting the fact that the signatureHashMap uses the hex-encoded fileHash as the grouping key, and if the hex encoding is case-insensitive but the comparison is case-sensitive, two signatures with the same hash but different case representations would be placed in different groups, splitting legitimate stake and allowing a fraudulent group to win?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a history reorganization by exploiting the fact that validate() does not verify that the StreamFileSignature objects in the collection were actually downloaded from the expected cloud storage bucket, allowing locally injected or replayed signatures to be mixed with legitimate ones and a fraudulent hash group to accumulate sufficient stake?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method does not enforce that each node contributes at most one signature per stream file, so that by replaying a single high-stake node's VERIFIED signature multiple times with the same fraudulent fileHash, the attacker can multiply that node's stake contribution and cause the fraudulent group to pass canReachConsensus()?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the mirror node to accept a tampered stream file by exploiting the fact that the validate() method does not verify the chain hash linking consecutive stream files, so that even if a fraudulent file passes consensus validation, the chain integrity check is not performed within validate() and the tampered file is accepted as part of the canonical history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a history reorganization by exploiting the fact that the validate() method does not check whether the consensusRatio configuration has been tampered with at runtime, and if an attacker can influence the Spring configuration (e.g., via an exposed actuator endpoint), setting consensusRatio to a very small value would lower the stakeRequiredForConsensus threshold and allow a minority-stake fraudulent group to pass?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the mirror node to accept a tampered stream file by exploiting the fact that the validate() method does not verify that the node reporting a given stake value is the same node that signed the file, allowing a node to claim a different node's stake when contributing to a fraudulent hash group?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a history reorganization by exploiting the fact that the validate() method does not verify the version field of the StreamFileSignature, allowing an attacker to replay old-format signatures (e.g., v2 without metadataHash) that were previously valid but correspond to a different (tampered) stream file content?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the mirror node to accept a tampered stream file by exploiting the fact that the validate() method does not check whether the VERIFIED signatures were produced by the correct cryptographic algorithm (SHA_384_WITH_RSA), and if a weaker algorithm is accepted upstream, a fraudulent signature could be forged and marked VERIFIED before reaching validate()?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a history reorganization by exploiting the fact that the validate() method does not enforce a minimum collection size, so that a collection containing a single VERIFIED signature from a node with stake >= stakeRequiredForConsensus would pass consensus and cause the mirror node to download and record a stream file based on a single node's attestation?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the mirror node to accept a tampered stream file by exploiting the fact that the validate() method does not verify that the fileHash in each signature matches the actual content of the downloaded stream file, so that even after consensus is reached on a fraudulent hash, the downstream file download and hash verification step could be bypassed if it is not implemented correctly?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidatorImpl.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a history reorganization by exploiting the fact that the validate() method does not check whether the signatures in the collection are for the same stream type (RECORD, BALANCE, EVENT), allowing signatures from different stream types to be mixed and a fraudulent hash group to accumulate cross-type stake?
